ECS 011.title: Artificial Intelligence for All

chapters.title: Deep Learning for Natural Language Processing
Philosophy, Ethics, and Safety of AI

textbooks.title: Human Compatible: Artificial Intelligence and the Problem of Control
Weapons of Math Destruction: How Big Data Increases Inequality and Threatens Democracy

chapters.title: Bomb Parts: What Is a Model?
Civilian Casualties: Justice in the Age of Big Data

ECS 011.notes: ECS 011 is a broad, non-programming introductory AI literacy course for general undergraduates. It likely relies heavily on lecture slides and curated readings rather than a single textbook. The Russell & Norvig text serves as a conceptual reference for technical foundations, while Russell's 'Human Compatible' and O'Neil's 'Weapons of Math Destruction' support the ethics and societal implications components. Instructors may substitute or supplement with freely available online readings, news articles, or course-specific notes.

ECS 012.title: Introduction to Media Computation

textbooks.title: Introduction to Computing and Programming in Python: A Multimedia Approach

chapters.title: Introduction to Computer Science and Media Computation
Modifying Pictures by Shifting, Mirroring, and Blurring

textbooks.title: Python Programming: An Introduction to Computer Science

ECS 012.notes: ECS 012 at UC Davis closely follows Guzdial & Ericson's media computation curriculum, which was specifically designed for non-CS majors. The course uses Python (often via JES - Jython Environment for Students) to manipulate images, sound, and text. The Zelle textbook serves as a supplementary Python programming reference.

ECS 017.title: Data, Logic, & Computing

chapters.title: Basic Structures: Sets, Functions, Sequences, Sums, and Matrices

textbooks.title: Code: The Hidden Language of Computer Hardware and Software

ECS 017.notes: ECS 017 is an introductory breadth course blending discrete mathematics with digital data representation and social context. It often relies on a combination of instructor-prepared lecture notes and excerpts from multiple texts rather than a single adopted textbook. Rosen's Discrete Mathematics is the closest match for the logic/proof/counting topics, while Petzold's Code and Schneider & Gersting's Invitation to Computer Science cover the data representation, digital logic, and societal impact components.

chapters.title: Basic Structures: Sets, Functions, Sequences, Sums

ECS 189N.notes: Special topics course with variable content each offering. Textbooks listed are representative for the Bioinformatics and Computational Biology area; actual course readings depend on the specific topic chosen by the instructor.

ECS 190C.title: Research Group Conferences in Computer Science
ECS 190C.notes: Research group conference course consisting of discussion sessions supervised by a faculty member. No fixed textbooks; readings are selected by the research group supervisor based on current research topics. Students consult primary literature (journal and conference papers) relevant to their research group.

ECS 190X.title: Senior Seminar
ECS 190X.notes: Senior seminar course examining a special topic in a small group setting. No fixed textbooks; readings vary by topic and instructor each offering. Students are expected to engage with current research papers, case studies, and instructor-selected materials appropriate to the semester's theme.

ECS 191.title: Software Design Project

textbooks.title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

ECS 191.notes: Project-based capstone course; textbooks serve as reference material for software process and design patterns. Primary deliverables are a large-scale team software project, design documents, and project presentations.

ECS 192.title: Internship in Computer Science
ECS 192.notes: Supervised industry internship for academic credit. No assigned textbook; students apply prior coursework skills in a professional workplace setting.

ECS 193A.title: Capstone Project
ECS 193A.notes: First half of senior design capstone. No assigned textbook; student teams respond to real client design challenges under faculty supervision.

ECS 193B.title: Capstone Project
ECS 193B.notes: Continuation and completion of senior design capstone (ECS 193A). No assigned textbook; teams implement and evaluate their client projects.

ECS 197T.title: Tutoring in Computer Science
ECS 197T.notes: Peer tutoring practicum for introductory CS courses. No assigned textbook; tutors draw on their own coursework knowledge and pedagogical guidance from the supervising instructor.

ECS 198.title: Directed Group Study
ECS 198.notes: Directed group study on a topic determined by the supervising instructor each offering. No fixed textbook; readings vary by topic.

ECS 198F.title: Student Facilitated Course
ECS 198F.notes: Student-facilitated course on a topic of the facilitator's choosing. No fixed textbook; content and materials are determined by the student facilitator under faculty oversight.

ECS 199.title: Special Study for Advanced Undergraduates
ECS 199.notes: Independent special study for advanced undergraduates. No fixed textbook; readings and deliverables are negotiated individually with the supervising faculty member.

ECS 199FA.title: Student Facilitated Course Development
ECS 199FA.notes: Course-development practicum in which a student designs the curriculum they will later teach under ECS 198F. No fixed textbook; the student selects and organizes their own course materials under faculty supervision.

ECS 199FB.title: Student Facilitated Teaching
ECS 199FB.notes: Teaching practicum in which a student delivers the course they developed in ECS 199FA. No fixed textbook; materials were determined during course development.

ECS 228.notes: This course focuses on applied cryptographic protocols for e-commerce: content distribution, payment systems, privacy and anonymity, and fair exchange. The primary text covers the necessary theoretical foundations; applied protocol details are supplemented with the HAC reference and research papers.

textbooks.title: Introduction to the Theory of Computation

ECS 232.notes: This course emphasizes formal models of chemical reaction networks, DNA strand displacement circuits, DNA self-assembly, and connections to distributed computing theory. Research papers by Winfree, Seeman, Qian, and others are central to the curriculum alongside the primary text.

chapters.title: Protocols for Authentication and Key Establishment

ECS 235B.notes: This course covers theoretical foundations: formal security models (Bell-LaPadula, Biba, Chinese Wall), non-interference, information flow, and the confinement problem. It complements ECS 235A by replacing applied system coverage with formal proofs of security properties.

ECS 289A.title: Special Topics in Computer Science: Computer Science Theory
ECS 289A.notes: Special topics course with variable content in CS Theory; textbooks and readings are assigned by the instructor and differ each offering. Common references depending on topic: Sipser 'Introduction to the Theory of Computation' 3rd ed. (computability, complexity); Arora & Barak 'Computational Complexity: A Modern Approach' (complexity theory); Vazirani 'Approximation Algorithms' (approximation). Consult the course syllabus for the current term's references.

ECS 289B.title: Special Topics in Computer Science: Architecture
ECS 289B.notes: Special topics course with variable content in Computer Architecture; textbooks are assigned by the instructor each offering. Common references depending on topic: Hennessy & Patterson 'Computer Architecture: A Quantitative Approach' 6th ed. (general architecture); Patterson & Waterman 'The RISC-V Reader' (ISA design); current IEEE/ACM ISCA/MICRO/HPCA proceedings. Consult the syllabus for the current term's references.

ECS 289C.title: Special Topics in Computer Science: Programming Languages & Compilers
ECS 289C.notes: Special topics course with variable content in Programming Languages and Compilers; textbooks are assigned by the instructor each offering. Common references depending on topic: Pierce 'Types and Programming Languages' (type theory); Aho, Lam, Sethi & Ullman 'Compilers: Principles, Techniques, and Tools' 2nd ed. (compilers); Harper 'Practical Foundations of Mathematics' (PL theory). Consult the syllabus for current term references.

ECS 089K.title: Special Topics in Computer Science: Scientific Computing
ECS 089K.notes: Textbook selection may vary by instructor; this entry reflects the Scientific Computing topic variant (ECS 089K). The Heath text aligns with the typical curriculum for scientific computing special topics.

ECS 089L.title: Special Topics in Computer Science: Computer Science
ECS 089L.notes: Special topics course covering variable CS subjects; textbooks are chosen by the instructor for each offering and cannot be assigned generically. May be repeated when topic differs.

ECS 092.title: Internship in Computer Science
ECS 092.notes: Supervised work experience course. No assigned textbooks; students complete an industry or research internship and submit a report.

ECS 098.title: Directed Group Study
ECS 098.notes: Directed group study course; readings and materials are determined by the supervising instructor based on the specific research or study topic chosen by the group.

ECS 098F.title: Student Facilitated Course
ECS 098F.notes: Student-facilitated course for lower division students. Materials and readings are selected by the student facilitator in consultation with a supervising instructor.

ECS 099.title: Special Study for Lower Division Students
ECS 099.notes: Independent special study for lower division students; readings and scope are determined individually by the supervising instructor.

ECS 111.title: Applied Machine Learning for Non-Majors

textbooks.title: Hands-On Machine Learning with Scikit-Learn, Keras, and TensorFlow

ECS 111.notes: Accessible ML course for non-CS/CSE majors (primarily Data Science majors). Emphasizes practical application over mathematical theory. No credit if ECS 171 has been taken.

ECS 113.title: Computer Security for Non-Majors
ECS 113.notes: Introductory security course for non-CS/CSE majors. Emphasis on practical security concepts and everyday applications rather than deep technical implementation.

ECS 115.title: Computer Networks for Non-Majors
ECS 115.notes: Introductory networking course for non-CS/CSE majors. Covers overview of networks, WWW, email, LANs, WANs, and TCP/IP without deep implementation focus.

ECS 116.title: Databases for Non-Majors
ECS 116.notes: Intro database course for non-CS/CSE majors. Covers ER design, relational model, SQL, indexing concepts, transaction basics, and NoSQL overview. No credit for students who completed ECS 165A or ECS 165B.

ECS 117.title: Algorithms for Data Science

chapters.title: Python Language Basics, IPython, and Jupyter Notebooks
NumPy Basics: Arrays and Vectorized Computation

ECS 117.notes: Practical algorithms course for Data Science students covering searching, pattern matching, clustering, and time series analysis. No credit if ECS 122A has been taken.

ECS 119.title: Data Processing Pipelines
ECS 119.notes: Covers software systems for processing large datasets including scripting, data streams, distributed computing, and deployment infrastructure. Prerequisite is ECS 116 or ECS 165A.

textbooks.title: Introduction to the Theory of Computation
Introduction to Automata Theory, Languages, and Computation

ECS 122B.title: Algorithm Design & Analysis

chapters.title: PSPACE: A Class of Problems Beyond NP

ECS 122B.notes: Continuation of ECS 122A. Focus on hard problems: NP-completeness, approximation algorithms, randomized algorithms, dynamic programming, branch and bound, and parallel/string/graph/geometric algorithms.

textbooks.title: Bioinformatics Algorithms: An Active Learning Approach

ECS 129.title: Computational Structural Bioinformatics

textbooks.title: Biological Sequence Analysis: Probabilistic Models of Proteins and Nucleic Acids

chapters.title: Prediction of Protein Structure and Function

ECS 129.notes: Covers protein and nucleic acid structure, structure comparison, structure prediction, molecular simulations, and structural bioinformatics databases.

ECS 130.title: Scientific Computation
ECS 130.notes: MATLAB-intensive course covering matrix-vector methods for numerical computation. Includes parallel computing for matrix operations.

ECS 132.title: Probability & Statistical Modeling for Computer Science

textbooks.title: Probability and Computing: Randomization and Probabilistic Techniques in Algorithms and Data Analysis

ECS 132.notes: CS-focused probability and statistics. Covers univariate and multivariate distributions, estimation, Markov chains, and Hidden Markov Models. Applications to data mining, networks, security, and bioinformatics.

ECS 140A.title: Programming Languages
ECS 140A.notes: First course in programming language principles. Covers syntactic definition, variables, types, data abstraction, OO, scoping, parameter passing, exceptions, and non-imperative languages.

ECS 140B.title: Programming Languages

textbooks.title: The Formal Semantics of Programming Languages: An Introduction

ECS 140B.notes: Continuation of ECS 140A. Covers functional and logic paradigms, concurrent programming, key implementation issues, and programming language semantics.

ECS 274.title: Automated Deduction

textbooks.title: Logic in Computer Science: Modelling and Reasoning about Systems
Handbook of Practical Logic and Automated Reasoning

ECS 274.notes: Core graduate course on formal methods and automated theorem proving. Covers resolution-based and rewriting-based proof methods, decision procedures, inductive proofs, and applications to program verification.

ECS 275A.title: Advanced Computer Graphics

textbooks.title: Physically Based Rendering: From Theory to Implementation

ECS 275A.notes: First quarter of the two-part advanced graphics sequence. Emphasis on hidden surface methods, shading models, anti-aliasing, and classical rendering techniques.

ECS 275B.title: Advanced Computer Graphics

textbooks.title: Physically Based Rendering: From Theory to Implementation

ECS 275B.notes: Second quarter of the two-part advanced graphics sequence. Focus on current research in image synthesis, global illumination, geometric modeling, and visualization. Topics drawn heavily from recent SIGGRAPH papers.

ECS 276.title: Advanced Volume Visualization
ECS 276.notes: Focuses on interactive volume visualization techniques, covering both software and hardware acceleration. Prerequisites include ECS 177; students should have graphics programming experience.

ECS 289D.title: Special Topics in Computer Science: Operating Systems
ECS 289D.notes: Special topics course; specific topics and reading list vary by instructor and offering. The textbook listed reflects typical advanced OS coverage; actual assignments may draw from current research papers.

ECS 289E.title: Special Topics in Computer Science: Software Engineering
ECS 289E.notes: Special topics course; actual content and readings vary by instructor and offering. May cover advanced topics such as program analysis, formal verification, DevOps, or empirical software engineering.

ECS 289F.title: Special Topics in Computer Science: Databases
ECS 289F.notes: Special topics course; actual topics and readings vary by instructor and offering. May cover distributed databases, NoSQL systems, data warehousing, stream processing, or data-intensive systems.

ECS 289G.title: Special Topics in Computer Science: Artificial Intelligence
ECS 289G.notes: Special topics course; actual topics and readings vary by instructor and offering. May focus on a specific AI subfield such as machine learning, NLP, computer vision, planning, or knowledge representation.

ECS 289H.title: Special Topics in Computer Science: Computer Graphics
ECS 289H.notes: Special topics course in computer graphics; actual topics and readings vary by instructor and offering. May cover advanced rendering, geometry processing, animation, or GPU programming.

ECS 289I.title: Special Topics in Computer Science: Networks
ECS 289I.notes: Special topics course in computer networks; actual topics and readings vary by instructor and offering. May cover software-defined networking, wireless networks, datacenter networking, or network security.

ECS 289J.title: Special Topics in Computer Science: Computer-Aided Design

textbooks.title: Curves and Surfaces for CAGD: A Practical Guide

ECS 289J.notes: Special topics course in computer-aided design; actual topics and readings vary by instructor and offering. May cover geometric modeling, mesh processing, solid modeling, or CAD/CAM systems.

ECS 289K.title: Special Topics in Computer Science: Scientific Computing
ECS 289K.notes: Special topics course in scientific computing; actual topics and readings vary by instructor and offering. May cover parallel computing, sparse matrix methods, finite element methods, or high-performance computing.

ECS 289L.title: Special Topics in Computer Science: Computer Science
ECS 289L.notes: General special topics course with no fixed subject area; textbook and reading list are entirely determined by the instructor and vary widely by offering. Too broad to assign a representative textbook.

ECS 289M.title: Special Topics in Computer Science: Security

textbooks.title: Security Engineering: A Guide to Building Dependable Distributed Systems

ECS 289M.notes: Special topics course in computer security; actual topics and readings vary by instructor and offering. May cover network security, cryptography, systems security, web security, or formal security models.

ECS 289N.title: Special Topics in Bioinformatics & Computational Biology

textbooks.title: Biological Sequence Analysis: Probabilistic Models of Proteins and Nucleic Acids

ECS 289N.notes: Special topics course in bioinformatics and computational biology; actual topics and readings vary by instructor and offering. May cover genomics, proteomics, single-cell analysis, or machine learning for biology.

ECS 290.title: Seminar in Computer Science
ECS 290.notes: Participating seminar covering current research and development in computer science. Readings consist of primary research literature (journal articles, conference papers) assigned by the instructor; no fixed textbook.

ECS 290C.title: Graduate Research Group Conference
ECS 290C.notes: Research group conference course focused on discussion of ongoing research problems and techniques. Readings are current research papers chosen by the research group; no fixed textbook.

ECS 293A.title: Research in Computer Science
ECS 293A.notes: First course in the PhD research methodology sequence. Covers research skills, ethics, finding research problems, and working with advisors. Readings are assigned by the instructor from papers and discipline-specific materials; no fixed textbook.

ECS 293B.title: Research in Computer Science
ECS 293B.notes: Second course in the PhD research methodology sequence. Focuses on presenting research results and communicating to the CS community. Readings are assigned by the instructor; no fixed textbook. Recommended after ECS 293A.

ECS 298.title: Group Study
ECS 298.notes: Directed group study on a topic determined by the instructor. Reading materials are chosen by the instructor to suit the specific topic of the group; no fixed textbook.

ECS 299.title: Research
ECS 299.notes: Individual research course for graduate students. No fixed textbook; reading materials are determined by the student and their research advisor based on the research project.

ECS 390.title: The Teaching of Computer Science
ECS 390.notes: TA practicum course. Covers leading discussion sections, writing and grading quizzes, and use of laboratory equipment. Pedagogy readings and materials are assigned by the instructor and vary by offering; no fixed textbook.

ECS 396.title: Teaching Assistant Training Practicum
ECS 396.notes: Structured TA training practicum. Activities and materials are determined by the supervising faculty and department TA coordinator; no fixed textbook.
